did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), a deep learning method, to perform segmentation of pigmented and non-pigmented skin lesions in clinical images acquired via smartphone cameras. The study evaluates the AI model's ability to correctly identify lesion contours, achieving approximately 92% accuracy on the test set.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing and evaluating a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) for skin lesion segmentation. It details specific methods (data acquisition using smartphone cameras, GAN training with/without rotation), reports quantitative results (91.4% accuracy on the test set), and includes sections on data acquisition, methodology, results, and conclusions, which is characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Generative Adversarial Neural Networks for Pigmented and Non-Pigmented Skin Lesions Detection in Clinical Images
journal: 21st International Conference on Control Systems and Computer Science
brief_summary: This study proposes and evaluates a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) for the segmentation of both pigmented and non-pigmented skin lesions in clinical images. The dataset consisted of high-resolution images acquired using smartphone cameras by laypersons, targeting applications in mHealth and eHealth. The researchers trained GAN models with and without image rotation to detect lesion contours, comparing their performance against the Otsu segmentation method. The primary evaluation metric was the percentage of correctly identified lesion contours on a test set. The GAN trained with image rotation achieved the highest accuracy of 91.4% after 200 epochs, outperforming the Otsu method which struggled with skin-colored and multi-colored lesions. The authors conclude that the proposed AI method is suitable for accurately detecting skin lesions and holds value for mobile dermatological screening applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on segmenting pigmented skin lesions, including those with multiple colors (e.g., multi-colour melanoma). The dataset includes images of pigmented lesions acquired via smartphone cameras.

The study explicitly targets non-pigmented (skin-colored) lesions, which are less commonly addressed in segmentation literature compared to pigmented ones. The GAN model was evaluated on its ability to segment these lesions, which often lack melanin and approach skin color.

Melanoma is mentioned as the most aggressive skin cancer and a key motivation for the study. Specific examples of segmented lesions include 'multi-colour melanoma' and references to achromic/amelanotic variants which are non-pigmented. However, the study does not perform a classification task distinguishing melanoma from other lesions, but rather segmentation of the lesion contour regardless of specific histopathology, though melanoma cases are present in the dataset.

BCC is mentioned in the introduction as one of the most common non-melanoma skin cancers and in references [3] and [4], but it is not explicitly stated that BCC cases were included in the specific dataset used for the GAN training and testing in this paper.

SCC is mentioned in the introduction as one of the most common non-melanoma skin cancers and in references [3] and [4], but it is not explicitly stated that SCC cases were included in the specific dataset used for the GAN training and testing in this paper.

all_task_types.target: Binary image mask marking the region of interest (lesion contour)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes and evaluates a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) for the segmentation of pigmented and non-pigmented skin lesions. The model predicts a binary mask where the lesion region is marked. The task is explicitly defined as 'lesion segmentation and contour extraction' and 'labeling' the skin condition via contour detection. The evaluation metric is the percentage of correctly identified lesion contours (accuracy), not a continuous regression value or a discrete diagnostic class label.

datasets_used.name: Proprietary Smartphone Dermatology Database
datasets_used.description: A proprietary database containing color clinical images of skin lesions acquired using smartphone cameras (iPhone 4S and higher, Samsung S4, S5, S6) by laypersons. The images were captured using a standardized mobile app to ensure high resolution (1920x1080) and focus, with the region of interest cropped to 407x408 pixels. The lesions were diagnosed by specialists via clinical inspection, dermoscopy, or histopathology. No preprocessing (contrast adjustment, sharpening, etc.) was applied.
classes_used: Non-melanoma cancer precursors (actinic keratosis, Bowen’s disease)

use.training: Used to train two GAN models (one without image rotation, one with image rotation) for lesion segmentation. The training set consisted of pairs of original images and manually identified binary masks.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the segmentation performance of the trained GAN models and the Otsu method. Accuracy was measured as the percentage of correctly identified lesion contours.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Percentage of correctly identified lesion contours

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (percentage of correctly identified lesion contours) and qualitative comparison against the Otsu method.

Proprietary Smartphone Dermatology Datab.metric: Percentage of correctly detected skin lesions (accuracy)

Otsu Segmentation Method.performance_description: Performed better than proposed methods for some small pigmented lesions, but failed in correctly identifying skin coloured or multi-coloured lesions in many cases.

Proprietary Smartphone Dermatology Datab.notes: The GAN trained with image rotation after 200 epochs achieved the best results, providing smoother contours, elimination of small surrounding skin patches, and complete segmentation. It dealt extremely well with lesions with a large range of shades (common in melanomas). The main problems appeared for extremely small or large lesions and lesions surrounded or covered by substantial hair. The overall percentage of correctly segmented lesions exceeded 90% when considering pigmented and non-pigmented skin lesions together.

architecture_details.discriminator: Not specified in detail, described as a learned loss function for the generative network
architecture_details.reference: Architecture derived from [27] (Isola et al., Image-to-image translation with conditional adversarial networks)

training_details.data_augmentation: Image rotation (two methods: without rotation and with rotation)
training_details.input_format: Pairs of original skin lesion images and binary image masks with the region of interest marked

classification_or_output_head.output: Binary image mask marking the lesion contour

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Active contour models (snake, gradient vector flow, Chan-Vese’s methods)
preprocessing_techniques: No preprocessing was applied to the images before the segmentation task (no contrast adjustment, sharpening, or other cleaning).
Image rotation data augmentation was applied during training (two methods: training without rotation and training with rotation).
Region of interest cropping to 407x408 pixels was performed by the mobile app during acquisition.
Images were acquired with a standardized mobile app to ensure high resolution (1920x1080) and focus, mitigating quality issues like shadows and reflections.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training as a comparative method. Two training configurations were used: one without rotation (images as acquired) and one with rotation (images rotated). The model trained with rotation achieved higher accuracy (91.4% at epoch 200) compared to the model without rotation (82.2% at epoch 200).

conclusions: The proposed Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) method is suitable for accurately detecting both pigmented and non-pigmented skin lesions, achieving a segmentation accuracy exceeding 90% on the test set.

The GAN model trained with image rotation achieved the highest performance (91.4% accuracy after 200 epochs), producing smoother contours and eliminating small surrounding skin patches compared to the model trained without rotation.
The GAN approach effectively handles lesions with a large range of shades, such as multi-colour melanomas, which are challenging for traditional methods.
The Otsu segmentation method performed better than the proposed GAN methods for some small pigmented lesions but failed to correctly identify skin-colored or multi-colored lesions in many cases.
The primary limitations of the GAN method include difficulties with extremely small or large lesions and lesions surrounded or covered by substantial hair.
The high accuracy obtained on images acquired by laypersons using smartphone cameras indicates the method's value for mHealth and eHealth dermatological screening applications.
Future work will focus on improving the GAN architecture, enlarging the dataset with more pigmented and non-pigmented lesions, and analyzing images in color spaces other than RGB.
important_remarks: The study uses a proprietary dataset of 3000 images acquired by laypersons via smartphone apps, which limits generalizability to clinical settings with professional dermatoscopes.

The dataset contains a mix of pigmented and non-pigmented lesions (including melanoma, BCC, SCC, and benign conditions), but the paper does not report per-class performance metrics, making it difficult to assess performance on specific high-risk classes like melanoma.
The manual ground truth labels used for training have 'low precision' because the manual segmentation encompasses the lesion and part of the surrounding skin, potentially introducing label noise.
No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on the internal test set (583 images) from the same proprietary database.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to compare the GAN performance against the Otsu method or between training configurations.
The GAN model trained without rotation showed performance degradation from epoch 100 (89.5%) to epoch 200 (82.2%), suggesting potential overfitting or instability in that configuration.
The method struggles with extremely small or large lesions and lesions surrounded or covered by substantial hair, which are common challenges in real-world mobile dermatology.

No preprocessing (contrast adjustment, sharpening, etc.) was applied to the images, relying on the standardized acquisition app to mitigate quality issues, which may limit robustness to uncontrolled imaging conditions.

The study is a conference paper (2017) with a relatively small dataset size compared to modern deep learning standards, and the architecture (U-Net based GAN) is based on earlier work without novel architectural contributions.
